Coverage is better when it explains the scientific payload, orbit and possible failure points instead of treating the event as pure spectacle. A successful launch is only the beginning of a much longer and more difficult mission. I prefer coverage that follows the mission after launch.

PART 1 · 4

Which planet interests you most?

Suggested phrasal verbs
send backdraw on
Model answer · 89 words

Mars interests me most because it is both familiar and alien. Rovers send back detailed landscapes, and researchers draw on those images to study water, climate and geology. A Mars rover can work for years without exposing astronauts to radiation or long travel. I would still support a future Mars mission, but robotic exploration seems the sensible way to answer most scientific questions first. The reddish landscape also makes the planet unusually easy to imagine. The long communication delay also makes the rover’s independent navigation and decision-making especially impressive.

PART 1 · 5

Do satellites affect your daily life?

Suggested phrasal verbs
send upsend back
Model answer · 84 words

Satellites affect my life constantly even though I rarely notice them. Agencies and companies send up systems for navigation, communication and weather services. Those satellites send back or relay information used by maps, forecasts and emergency teams. navigation systems and communication satellites have become ordinary infrastructure. This is probably the strongest argument for space spending because the public benefit is direct, widespread and not dependent on an astronaut planting another flag. Many essential services would become unreliable within hours if those systems suddenly failed.

PART 1 · 6

Would you visit a space museum?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw onbuild up
Model answer · 79 words

I would visit one, especially if it included real spacecraft, mission control equipment and interactive explanations. Museums can draw on dramatic objects while also showing how teams build up knowledge through testing and failure. I would be interested in the contrast between early rockets and reusable systems. A good museum should also explain the role of technicians, mathematicians and international partners, because space history is often reduced to a few famous astronauts. Exhibitions should include failures and abandoned designs.

PART 1 · 7

Should children learn about space?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build upcarry out
Model answer · 88 words

Children should learn about space because it connects physics, geography, biology and engineering. Simple activities can build up curiosity before students encounter difficult mathematics. They can also carry out small observations of the Moon or satellites. However, lessons should not present exploration as a heroic story without costs or risks. Students should discuss space debris, Earth observation and the ethical questions surrounding resource extraction, so enthusiasm develops alongside critical thinking. Space education can also help students understand scale, evidence and the difference between scientific possibility and promotional speculation.

PART 1 · 8

Do you use satellite weather forecasts?

Suggested phrasal verbs
send backdraw on
Model answer · 84 words

I use weather apps every day, although I rarely think about the satellites behind them. Instruments send back measurements of clouds, temperature and storms, while forecasters draw on those records to build models. Reliable weather forecasting is a practical result of space infrastructure. It also supports farming, transport and disaster response. The service feels ordinary precisely because decades of public investment have made a technically complex system dependable. Long-term records matter because one satellite image is useful, but decades of comparable measurements reveal trends.

PART 1 · 9

Would you live on the Moon?

Suggested phrasal verbs
set upbuild up
Model answer · 85 words

I would not choose to live on the Moon permanently. Even if engineers set up a secure moon base, daily life would involve radiation, confined spaces and dependence on supplies. A settlement would need to build up reliable power, recycling and medical systems before families could live there. I might accept a short research visit, but the idea of viewing a lifeless landscape through a small window for years is less romantic than promotional images suggest. Psychological isolation may be as difficult as the engineering.

PART 1 · 10

Is space tourism a good idea?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scale upstep in
Model answer · 89 words

Space tourism may help companies scale up reusable technology and lower some launch costs. However, regulators should step in if safety, emissions or orbital congestion are treated as private matters. The experience currently serves a very small wealthy group, so claims about universal access are exaggerated. I do not oppose it completely, but commercial flights should pay for oversight and debris mitigation rather than relying on public agencies to manage every risk they create. Companies should also disclose the full environmental cost rather than presenting reusability as automatically sustainable.

PART 1 · 11

Do you follow Mars-rover news?

Suggested phrasal verbs
send backcarry out
Model answer · 89 words

I follow major discoveries rather than every daily update. A Mars rover can carry out geology, drill rocks and photograph distant terrain. The images it send back make the mission easier to understand than a table of measurements alone. I am especially interested in evidence about ancient water and habitable environments. Robotic missions are slow, but their patience is an advantage: they can investigate one region carefully without the life-support demands of human spaceflight. Sample collection and remote analysis can answer many questions before a crewed mission becomes necessary.

PART 1 · 12

Would you support a local space company?

Suggested phrasal verbs
spin offscale up
Model answer · 82 words

I would support a space company if it created useful engineering, Earth services or scientific instruments. Such firms can spin off materials and software into other industries and scale up ideas developed through public research. I would be less enthusiastic about a company based entirely on luxury tourism or speculative lunar mining. Public support should depend on measurable benefits, fair contracts and responsible treatment of orbital debris, not merely on impressive launch videos. Local benefits should include training and durable supply chains.

PART 1 · 13

Do space images change how you see Earth?

Suggested phrasal verbs
send backdraw on
Model answer · 84 words

Images of Earth from deep space make the planet appear both beautiful and vulnerable. Astronauts and spacecraft send back views without national borders, and communicators draw on them to discuss environmental responsibility. The emotional effect should not replace policy, but it can strengthen interest in climate monitoring and international cooperation. A distant image also reminds us that exploration and earthly responsibility are not opposites; space technology often helps us understand the planet we already inhabit. The image is powerful because it reduces political boundaries.

PART 1 · 14

Should countries cooperate in space?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build upmove ahead
Model answer · 89 words

Countries should cooperate because missions are expensive and technically demanding. Partnerships build up shared expertise and make abrupt political cancellation more costly. Agencies can move ahead with a broader base of funding and equipment. Cooperation also creates diplomatic value, as the space station demonstrated. However, agreements need clear responsibilities and fair access. Smaller partners should contribute meaningful science rather than serve only as symbolic passengers in projects controlled by larger powers. Shared missions can survive changes of government more easily when expertise and hardware are distributed among several partners.

PART 1 · 15

Would you study space science?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw onpay off
Model answer · 89 words

I would consider studying a part of space science connected to Earth data or planetary geology. These fields draw on physics, computing and environmental science, and they can pay off through careers beyond one agency. I would probably prefer robotic exploration or Earth observation to astronautics because the research has a clear scientific focus. The mathematics would be challenging, but the opportunity to work with global datasets and international teams would be attractive. The field could also connect planetary discoveries with climate research and the history of Earth itself.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

Should governments spend billions on human spaceflight?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move aheadpay off
Model answer · 128 words

Governments should fund human spaceflight selectively rather than treating every crewed objective as automatically valuable. A mission may pay off through technology, diplomacy and public inspiration, but astronauts require expensive life-support and return systems. Agencies should move ahead only when humans add value that robotic exploration cannot provide. cost-benefit analysis must compare the same scientific goal across different mission designs. The debate is not space versus hospitals in a simplistic sense; all public programmes have an opportunity cost. Crewed exploration can deserve support, but budgets, risks and alternatives should be explained through transparent communication. Public agencies should publish independent comparisons before committing to decades of infrastructure, because symbolic ambition can make later cancellation politically impossible. Independent parliamentary review can keep a programme aligned with its stated public purpose.

PART 3 · 2

Is robotic exploration better than sending astronauts?

Suggested phrasal verbs
carry outsend back
Model answer · 124 words

Robots are usually better for dangerous, distant and highly focused scientific work. They can carry out long observations and send back data without food, oxygen or a return vehicle. A Mars rover can operate for years at a fraction of the risk of a crewed expedition. Humans remain more adaptable, can repair equipment and make rapid field decisions. Their presence also creates political and cultural attention that robotic missions rarely achieve. The correct choice depends on the objective. evidence-based policymaking should use robots for most preliminary exploration and reserve astronauts for tasks where flexibility, construction or direct judgement justifies the additional mission costs. Humans may still be necessary for complex construction or rapid geological interpretation, but that claim should be demonstrated mission by mission.

PART 3 · 3

How does space exploration benefit life on Earth?

Suggested phrasal verbs
spin offsend back
Model answer · 128 words

The clearest benefits come from satellites. Earth-observation missions send back information for weather forecasting, climate monitoring, agriculture and disaster response. Navigation and communication systems support almost every modern economy. Exploration can also spin off sensors, materials and software, although agencies sometimes exaggerate these indirect benefits. The deeper contribution is institutional: difficult missions develop engineering teams and scientific methods that produce knowledge spillovers. Nevertheless, benefits should be demonstrated rather than assumed. A spectacular moon landing does not automatically improve social welfare. Programmes create long-term public value when scientific data, technology and infrastructure remain accessible beyond the original mission. Open access to satellite records is particularly important because data locked behind expensive contracts cannot deliver equitable public value. Agencies should publish how frequently the information is used in operational decisions.

PART 3 · 4

Should Earth problems take priority over Mars missions?

Suggested phrasal verbs
carry outmove ahead
Model answer · 127 words

Earthly problems deserve priority in the sense that governments must guarantee basic services and climate resilience. However, public budgets are not a single choice between one Mars mission and all earthly priorities. Governments can carry out a limited exploration programme while continuing to fund health, housing and education. Space science also contributes to Earth through satellites and technological capacity. The key question is scale and purpose. Agencies should not move ahead with open-ended settlement plans without clear science, realistic costs and democratic accountability. Exploration is defensible as strategic investment, but it becomes irresponsible when prestige overrides urgent needs or when budget overruns destroy more productive missions. A responsible programme should also protect Earth-science budgets from being repeatedly raided to rescue prestige missions that exceed their original cost.

PART 3 · 5

Why are countries returning to the Moon?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build upset up
Model answer · 119 words

Countries are returning because the Moon combines science, technology, strategy and prestige. Missions can build up experience in navigation, landing and life-support before a more distant Mars mission. Agencies also hope to set up infrastructure near the lunar south pole, where water ice may support future activity. Geopolitical competition matters as well: governments want strategic autonomy and influence over emerging lunar governance. Commercial firms see contracts and possible space resources. These motives are not necessarily illegitimate, but they require scrutiny. A new space race could accelerate innovation while also exporting military rivalry and unequal resource extraction beyond Earth. The public should understand whether a mission is primarily scientific, commercial or strategic, since different goals require different standards of success.

PART 3 · 6

Who should control lunar resources?

Suggested phrasal verbs
look intostep in
Model answer · 125 words

No company or nation should gain permanent control simply because it arrives first. Existing space law prohibits national ownership of celestial bodies, yet rules for commercial extraction remain contested. International institutions should look into licensing, environmental zones and benefit sharing before large-scale lunar mining begins. Governments must step in because commercial incentives favour rapid extraction, while damage to scientifically important sites may be irreversible. A workable regulatory framework should protect access, require disclosure and establish liability. public consultation is also necessary because transforming the Moon is a civilisational decision, not merely an engineering contract between agencies and private firms. Agreements should also protect culturally significant landscapes and preserve untouched reference sites for future science. Liability rules must remain enforceable even when a company changes ownership.

PART 3 · 7

How serious is the space-debris problem?

Suggested phrasal verbs
phase outstep in
Model answer · 128 words

space debris is already a serious infrastructure risk. Even small fragments travel fast enough to damage satellites, while crowded orbits increase the need for collision avoidance. Operators should phase out designs that cannot be safely removed at the end of a mission. Regulators must step in when companies launch large constellations without credible disposal plans. debris mitigation can prevent new objects, but active removal may be required for dangerous existing debris. The problem illustrates a classic shared-resource failure: each operator benefits from orbit, while the costs of pollution are distributed. orbital sustainability therefore requires binding standards and shared responsibility. Insurance and licensing should make operators responsible for end-of-life disposal instead of transferring clean-up costs to future users. International standards are more effective than voluntary promises made after launch.

PART 3 · 8

Should private companies lead space exploration?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scale upcarry out
Model answer · 121 words

Private companies can scale up launch production, reduce some costs and carry out contracts more flexibly than public agencies. Reusable rockets show how competition can change an industry. However, companies pursue profit and may abandon projects when returns weaken. Fundamental planetary science, public data and planetary defense cannot depend entirely on commercial markets. The best arrangement is a carefully designed public-private model: governments define public goals, fund high-risk research and enforce safety, while firms deliver systems competitively. Contracts need democratic accountability, transparent pricing and protection against dependency on one supplier. Commercial leadership is useful, but public purpose must remain visible. Governments should maintain enough internal expertise to evaluate contractors rather than becoming dependent on the firms they are supposed to supervise.

PART 3 · 9

Can space exploration unite countries?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build upmove ahead
Model answer · 127 words

Space cooperation can build up trust because missions require years of shared technical work. The space station showed that countries could move ahead with a common programme despite political disagreements. Joint missions distribute cost, combine specialised equipment and support international collaboration. Yet space can also intensify rivalry through military satellites, prestige competition and control of launch capacity. Cooperation is therefore not automatic. Agreements need stable funding, data sharing and fair decision-making. Projects designed only for symbolic unity may collapse under political pressure, while genuine international cooperation is strongest when each partner contributes essential capabilities and receives meaningful scientific access. Cooperation is also vulnerable when one partner controls essential transport, so diversified launch and communication capability improves resilience. Scientific cooperation should also protect open data and researcher mobility.

PART 3 · 10

Is a permanent moon base realistic?

Suggested phrasal verbs
set upbuild up
Model answer · 122 words

A small research outpost may be realistic, but a self-sufficient city is far more difficult. Agencies can set up temporary habitats and build up experience in power, recycling and surface operations. However, the lunar surface exposes crews to radiation, dust, extreme temperatures and logistical dependence. Every replacement part must initially come from Earth. A permanent moon base also requires stable budgets across several governments and decades. Technical feasibility is only one question; opportunity cost, environmental protection and purpose matter too. An outpost linked to clear science may be justified, whereas settlement for prestige alone would be difficult to defend. A staged programme with explicit review points would allow useful research to continue without converting every experiment into a promise of permanent settlement.

PART 3 · 11

Should space agencies prioritise Earth observation?

Suggested phrasal verbs
send upsend back
Model answer · 121 words

Space agencies should protect Earth observation as a core mission because satellites send back data used for weather, climate, agriculture and emergencies. Governments often celebrate deep-space launches while treating Earth science as less glamorous. Yet continuous records are difficult to replace once a satellite fails. Agencies should send up successor instruments before gaps appear. This is a strong case for mission-driven research with clear public benefit. Earth observation should not consume every science budget, but it deserves stable funding because it supports daily services and evidence-based policymaking. Exploring other worlds and monitoring our own planet should be complementary rather than rivals. Earth-science data should also be translated into usable tools for local governments, farmers and emergency services rather than merely archived.

PART 3 · 12

What is the value of planetary defence?

Suggested phrasal verbs
look intostep in
Model answer · 124 words

planetary defense addresses a low-probability but potentially catastrophic risk. Scientists look into near-Earth objects, improve asteroid detection and calculate future trajectories. Because no private customer can capture the full benefit of protecting Earth, governments must step in. The programme also requires international data sharing because an impact would ignore national borders. Critics may question spending on rare events, but early detection is relatively modest compared with the consequences of an undiscovered large asteroid. A sensible risk assessment should support sustained surveys, response planning and research into deflection rather than dramatic last-minute projects. Governments should prepare communication and civil-defence protocols in advance, because detection without an agreed response could create confusion. Regular international exercises could test whether detection systems and emergency agencies can coordinate effectively.

PART 3 · 13

Does commercial space tourism have social value?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scale uppay off
Model answer · 122 words

Commercial tourism may help firms scale up reusable vehicles, and some investment may pay off through safer launch systems or research access. However, the direct experience is currently limited to extremely wealthy passengers. Claims that luxury flights serve humanity should therefore be treated cautiously. Tourism can also create emissions, noise and additional space traffic. A fair policy would require companies to cover regulation, emergency planning and environmental costs. The activity may have some technological value, but it is not equivalent to public scientific research. Its legitimacy depends on whether commercial gains support broader access and responsible orbital sustainability. A small research market may emerge, but it should not be used as moral cover for an industry whose main product is elite entertainment.

PART 3 · 14

How should space missions be evaluated?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw oncarry out
Model answer · 125 words

Mission evaluation should draw on scientific output, technical learning, cost, schedule and public use of data. A programme must also continue on promised archives and technology transfer after the dramatic launch is over. Because “follow through” is not in this chapter’s phrasal set, the practical principle is still important: agencies should carry out reviews that do not measure success only by reaching a destination. measurable outcomes may include publications, operational services, trained personnel and international capability. Evaluation should also examine broader social costs and whether the same goal could have been achieved robotically. A balanced framework protects ambition without allowing symbolic success to hide weak performance. Long-term evaluation is essential because infrastructure and scientific skills may remain valuable even when one original mission objective changes.

PART 3 · 15

What principles should guide future space exploration?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move aheaddraw on
Model answer · 118 words

Future exploration should move ahead under principles of scientific value, sustainability and public accountability. Agencies should draw on decades of technical experience while accepting that permanent settlements and extraction create new ethical questions. Missions need credible risk assessment, open data and realistic budgets. lunar governance should be negotiated internationally before commercial activity becomes irreversible. Operators must protect orbital sustainability and avoid treating space as an unlimited waste site. Finally, exploration should complement earthly priorities through Earth services, shared technology and international cooperation. Ambition is valuable, but ambition without rules merely exports familiar human problems to a larger stage. A precautionary approach is especially important where damage to orbital or lunar environments may be difficult or impossible to reverse.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Human missions to Mars are too expensive and risky to justify public funding. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
Mars missionhuman spaceflightrobotic explorationcarry outsend backbuild upopportunity costcrewed missionlunar surface
Model essay · 347 words

A human journey to Mars would be one of the most difficult projects ever attempted. Although the scientific and cultural value could be considerable, I largely agree that public funding is difficult to justify until the technology becomes safer and the mission has a clear purpose.

The main concern is cost. A Mars mission requires life-support, radiation shielding, food, medical systems and a return vehicle. These needs make human spaceflight far more expensive than robotic exploration. Rovers can carry out geology for years and send back detailed measurements without risking a crew. What a human mission must demonstrate is that astronauts can achieve something proportionate to the additional expense. Risk is equally serious. Communication delays prevent immediate assistance, while equipment failure may leave no rescue option. Were a crew to face a major medical emergency, Earth-based specialists could provide advice but not direct treatment. Public agencies must therefore avoid schedules driven mainly by prestige.

Supporters argue that astronauts can improvise, collect samples quickly and inspire new generations. A mission may also build up technology useful for lunar operations and long-duration medicine. These benefits matter, but they can be developed through staged research before committing to a landing. Governments should fund robotic missions, propulsion, closed-loop life support and radiation research. Independent review should then compare scientific value, safety and opportunity cost. International cooperation could distribute expense and reduce nationalistic competition.

A mission lasting several years would need political support across multiple governments and budget cycles. If funding weakened after launch, the crew could not simply pause the project. This makes transparent international agreements and contingency reserves essential. Robotic exploration is less politically fragile because failure, while expensive, does not immediately threaten human life. The first crewed mission should therefore be treated as the final stage of a long evidence-building programme rather than the opening move of a political race.

In conclusion: a human Mars programme may become defensible, but current public commitments should focus on technology and robotic science. A crewed landing is justified only when its purpose exceeds symbolic achievement and its risks are no longer experimental.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe private companies should lead future space exploration, while others think national space agencies should remain in control. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
scale upreusable rocketslaunch costscommercial incentivesspace resourcesplanetary scienceEarth observationplanetary defensepublic-private model
Model essay · 321 words

Private companies have transformed launch markets, leading some people to argue that they should direct the next era of exploration. Others believe public agencies must retain control because space activity serves strategic and scientific goals. In my view, companies should deliver services competitively, while governments define priorities and rules.

Commercial firms can move quickly, scale up production and accept technical risk. reusable rockets have lowered some launch costs, and private suppliers can design vehicles without the same administrative structure as a public agency. Competition may therefore accelerate innovation. However, companies follow commercial incentives. They may abandon unprofitable science, prioritise tourism or seek control over space resources. Fundamental planetary science, Earth observation and planetary defense provide broad benefits that are difficult to sell to individual customers. What markets cannot reliably provide is a complete system organised around the public interest.

National agencies also maintain scientific expertise and represent democratic authority. Nevertheless, they can become slow, politically unstable and dependent on prestige missions. The best approach is a regulated public-private model. Governments should set mission objectives, own essential data and enforce safety; firms should compete to provide launchers, landers and communications. Not only must contracts disclose costs and performance, but agencies must also retain enough institutional capacity to evaluate suppliers. Dependence on one company would weaken accountability.

Public ownership of data is equally important. When taxpayers fund a mission, scientific measurements should normally remain openly available. Commercial secrecy may be justified for limited engineering details, but it should not prevent independent evaluation. Governments should also diversify contractors so one technical failure or corporate dispute cannot halt an entire national programme. Public agencies also need procurement rules that prevent contractors from using public dependence to renegotiate costs after competitors have disappeared.

In conclusion: private companies should play a leading operational role, but they should not determine the purpose or legal framework of exploration. Public institutions must remain responsible for science, sustainability and democratic choice.

TASK 2 · 3

Large satellite constellations provide communication services but increase congestion in orbit. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
satellite constellationclimate monitoringdisaster responsespace trafficcollision avoidanceorbital debrisdebris mitigationorbital sustainabilityhuman spaceflight
Model essay · 300 words

Large satellite constellations can provide broadband, navigation and frequent Earth imagery. Although these services offer important advantages, I believe they outweigh the disadvantages only when strict orbital rules are enforced.

The main advantage is coverage. A satellite constellation can connect remote communities and support emergency communication. Frequent observations may improve climate monitoring, agriculture and disaster response. Lower production and launch costs can also make services more affordable. The disadvantage is congestion. Thousands of satellites increase space traffic and the need for collision avoidance. Failed spacecraft may become orbital debris, while bright constellations can interfere with astronomy. What appears to one company as a manageable launch plan may create collective risk when many operators act simultaneously.

Governments should require reliable manoeuvring, short disposal deadlines and financial guarantees for end-of-life removal. Operators must share tracking data and coordinate internationally. Only when companies pay for debris mitigation can commercial expansion be considered responsible. Regulators should also assess effects on astronomy and remote communities rather than accepting promises of universal connectivity. The social benefit depends on access. If services remain expensive, public orbital resources will mainly support wealthy customers. Governments may therefore attach coverage and affordability conditions to licences.

Astronomy must also be protected. Bright satellites can affect observations, while radio transmissions may interfere with sensitive instruments. Operators should cooperate with observatories during design rather than offering minor adjustments after deployment. A constellation that provides communication but weakens humanity’s ability to study the universe creates another policy trade-off that licensing authorities must evaluate carefully. Annual reports should publish failure rates, disposal performance and access prices so benefits are assessed through evidence rather than promotional claims.

In conclusion: satellite constellations can create major communication and Earth-observation benefits. Their advantages exceed the disadvantages only within a binding framework that protects orbital sustainability and distributes access fairly.

TASK 2 · 4

Space debris is increasing rapidly. What problems does this cause, and what measures should governments and companies introduce?

Optional collocation bank
space debriscollision avoidancedebris mitigationphase outactive removalshared responsibilityhuman spaceflightrobotic explorationcrewed mission
Model essay · 306 words

The amount of space debris is increasing as launches and satellite constellations expand. This creates risks for science, communication and future exploration.

The first problem is collision. Small fragments travel at extremely high speed and can damage active satellites or crewed vehicles. Operators must perform more collision avoidance, consuming fuel and staff time. A major collision could create thousands of new fragments and make valuable orbits increasingly difficult to use. A second problem is responsibility. Old objects may belong to governments or companies that no longer operate, while current law restricts who can remove them. What the orbital environment lacks is a clear system in which polluters finance prevention and clean-up.

Governments should require spacecraft to meet debris mitigation standards before launch. Designs need passivation, reliable disposal and safe re-entry. Licensing agencies can demand insurance or deposits returned only after successful removal. Unsafe designs should be phase out. Existing debris may require active removal. International agreements should define consent, liability and priority objects. Tracking data should be shared openly, and commercial operators should contribute to a common fund.

Had strong disposal rules existed during earlier decades, today’s congestion might have been significantly lower. Preventing new debris remains cheaper than cleaning orbit later. Better economic incentives are also required. At present, responsible disposal may cost an operator money while the benefit is shared by everyone. A common levy based on collision risk could reward safer designs and finance tracking or removal. This would convert shared responsibility from an attractive principle into a practical system with predictable funding. Countries with major launch industries should contribute more because their commercial activity creates a larger share of future tracking and removal costs.

In conclusion: debris threatens essential infrastructure and the long-term use of space. Binding design standards, transparent tracking and internationally coordinated removal are necessary before orbital pollution becomes irreversible.

TASK 2 · 5

Why are several countries planning permanent activity on the Moon? What rules should govern future lunar development?

Optional collocation bank
strategic autonomyspace resourcesbuild upmoon baseplanetary scienceMars missionresource extractionspace lawregulatory framework
Model essay · 300 words

Countries are planning permanent lunar activity for scientific, strategic and commercial reasons. The Moon can support astronomy and geology, while its lower gravity and possible water ice may assist later missions. Governments also seek prestige and strategic autonomy, and companies anticipate contracts or future space resources.

A permanent presence may build up experience in life-support, construction and surface operations. A moon base could support planetary science and serve as a staging point for a Mars mission. However, these goals also introduce military rivalry and resource extraction. Rules should be established before large-scale development. Existing space law does not clearly resolve ownership of extracted materials or long-term exclusion zones. Only through international agreement can the Moon avoid becoming a race in which early arrival creates practical control.

A future regulatory framework should protect scientifically important regions, require environmental assessment and limit interference with other missions. Companies need licences, transparent contracts and liability for damage. public consultation should influence decisions because lunar transformation is partly financed by citizens. The rules should also preserve access. No country should reserve the most valuable sites indefinitely without active use or scientific justification. A shared database of infrastructure and planned operations could reduce conflict.

Military activity requires particular attention. Scientific bases and communication systems may also have strategic uses, making verification difficult. States should disclose major infrastructure, avoid weapons deployment and create inspection procedures. Without such safeguards, a civilian research outpost could gradually become part of a security competition that makes cooperation and open science harder. International scientific organisations could help identify protected reference sites before commercial infrastructure changes them permanently worldwide.

In conclusion: states are returning to the Moon because it offers knowledge, technology and strategic influence. Future lunar governance must combine open access, environmental caution and accountability before temporary missions become permanent territorial control.

Course index · Module 1 · Chapter 09

Academic English Studio · Topic 09 · Space Exploration and Earthly Priorities


Saved automatically on this device.
